claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 6 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 6 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 6 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital/residential accommodation for 13 weeks (>12) and claimant is not regularly spending money on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To begin assessing your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to know about the child or children you're claiming for.

**How old is the child you're claiming Child Benefit for?**

Please let me know if they are:
- **Under 16 years old**
- **Between 16 and 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children, we can go through each one - just let me know their ages.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi there! Yes, I'm interested in claiming Child Benefit. I have two children - Alex who is 6 years old, and Blake who is 3 years old.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful! Thank you for that information. Since both Alex (6 years old) and Blake (3 years old) are under 16, they both automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Since Alex (6) and Blake (3) are both young children, I can safely assume they are not working 24 or more hours per week. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This refers to benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit that the children themselves would be claiming. For young children like yours, this is typically not the case, but I need to confirm.

Are both children free from receiving such benefits in their own name?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them receive any benefits in their own names. They're just young kids!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's exactly what I expected for young children! Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Since Alex (6) and Blake (3) are young children, they clearly aren't in any apprenticeship programmes. Let me move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check your residency status. 

**Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You qualify if **any one** of these applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. **You have pre-settled status** and are actively looking for work
5. **You have pre-settled status** with sufficient resources to support yourself and your family
6. **You have pre-settled status** and are studying with sufficient resources
7. **You have pre-settled status** as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with the right to reside
8. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

Do any of these apply to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm based in Manchester with my kids.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's perfect! Living in the UK meets the residency requirement. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You're considered responsible for a child if **any one** of these applies:

1. **You live with the child** - The child lives with you
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** - You pay at least the equivalent of the Child Benefit amount towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **You pay maintenance** - Under a court order or agreement that covers the child's upkeep

Since you mentioned you're based in Manchester with your kids, I assume they both live with you - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - both Alex and Blake live at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That confirms you're responsible for both children. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Another family member

If so, is there any dispute about who should receive the benefit, or has there been an agreement about transferring the claim to you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's clear. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Where are Alex and Blake currently living?**

I need to check if any special circumstances apply. Are the children:

- **In hospital** (for an extended stay)
- **In local authority care**
- **In a fostering arrangement**
- **Living normally at home** with you

I assume they're both living normally at home with you in Manchester - is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex lives at home with me normally. But Blake has been in hospital for a few months now.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear Blake has been in hospital. I hope they're getting the care they need and will be home soon.

Since the children have different circumstances, let me check each one separately.

**For Alex (living normally at home):**

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
For Alex, I just need to confirm one more thing:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, the council isn't financially supporting Alex's living costs or care?

And I'll also need to check on Blake's situation. You mentioned Blake has been in hospital for a few months - **do you know roughly how long Blake has been in hospital?** This is important because there are specific rules about hospital stays.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
For Alex, no - the council isn't paying anything for him. He just lives at home with me.

As for Blake, it's been about 3 months now that he's been in the hospital.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. Let me check both situations.

**For Alex** - that's great, he meets all the requirements.

**For Blake** - I need to check the rules around hospital stays.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. I can see that **Alex is ELIGIBLE** for Child Benefit - great news!

Now for **Blake** who has been in hospital for about 3 months (approximately 12 weeks), I need to check a couple more things.

The rules state that Child Benefit continues for up to **12 weeks** when a child is in hospital. Since Blake is right around that 12-week mark, let me ask:

**Are you regularly spending money on Blake's needs while they're in hospital?**

This could include things like:
- Visiting them (travel costs)
- Buying them clothes, toys, or other items
- Paying for anything related to their care or comfort

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, I haven't been able to afford to spend money on their needs while they're in there. It's been really tough financially.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear about your financial difficulties. I understand this is a challenging time for your family.

I now have enough information to provide you with the eligibility outcome for both children.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## Child Benefit Eligibility Results

Here's a summary of the eligibility assessment for your two children:

### ✅ Alex (6 years old) - **ELIGIBLE**

Alex meets all the requirements for Child Benefit:
- Under 16 years old
- Lives with you at home
- No one else is claiming for him
- The local council isn't paying for his care

**Next steps for Alex:**
- Apply online or by post using form CH2
- Provide proof of identity for yourself and Alex
- Provide Alex's birth certificate
- Payments are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

---

### ❌ Blake (3 years old) - **NOT ELIGIBLE** (at this time)

Unfortunately, Blake is not currently eligible because:
- Blake has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks (around 3 months)
- Child Benefit normally stops after 12 weeks in hospital
- An exception exists if you're regularly spending money on the child while they're in hospital, but you've not been able to do this

**Important information for Blake:**
- **When Blake comes home**, you should be able to claim Child Benefit for them again
- If Blake leaves hospital and returns within 28 days, the time may count towards the 12-week limit, so keep track of any stays
- You may wish to contact HMRC directly to discuss your specific situation and any exceptions that might apply

---

### 💡 Additional Information

If you or a partner earns over **£60,000**, be aware of the High Income Child Benefit Charge - though this doesn't affect eligibility, it may affect how much you keep.

I'm really sorry to hear things have been financially tough. If you'd like, I can provide information about other support that might be available to you during this difficult time. Wishing Blake a speedy recovery! 💙

reasonings: Alex (6 years old) meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: 1) He is under 16 years old, 2) He is not working 24+ hours per week, 3) He does not receive benefits in his own right, 4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, 5) You (the claimant) live in the UK, 6) You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you, 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, 8) He lives normally at home, and 9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (3 years old) is unfortunately not eligible for Child Benefit at this time because: 1) Blake has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks (around 3 months), 2) Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks when a child is in hospital, 3) An exception to this 12-week rule exists if you are regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital, but you confirmed you have not been able to afford to do so. Therefore, the hospital stay rule means Child Benefit cannot continue for Blake while they remain in hospital without you regularly spending on their needs.
arguments.overall_reasoning: This family has two children with different eligibility outcomes. Alex, who lives at home normally, is fully eligible for Child Benefit. Blake, who has been hospitalised for approximately 3 months (around 12 weeks), is not currently eligible because the hospital stay exceeds the 12-week limit and the claimant is not regularly spending money on Blake's needs during the hospital stay. When Blake returns home, they should become eligible for Child Benefit again.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

response.question: Has the child's cumulative time in hospital or residential accommodation exceeded 12 weeks?
response.help_text: Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks in hospital/residential accommodation. CUMULATIVE RULE: If child returns within 28 days of leaving, time counts towards 12-week limit. Multiple stays within 28 days are added together.

criteria.calculation_method: Sum all hospital/residential stays. If gap between stays is less than 28 days, count both stays towards cumulative total. If gap is 28+ days, reset counter.
criteria.example: 10 weeks hospital + 15 day gap + 3 weeks hospital = 13 weeks cumulative (exceeds limit)

age_limits.birthday_rule: Benefit stops on child's 20th birthday, not end of academic year

training_exclusions: Training with employer agreement for future job

payment_rates.note: Current rates not specified in source documents - check gov.uk for latest rates

high_income_charge.note: High Income Child Benefit Charge applies if you or partner earns over £60,000. This is a payment adjustment, not an eligibility criterion.

response.question: Are you regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital/residential accommodation?
response.help_text: Exception to 12-week rule if you regularly spend money on child. For hospital abroad, only if they went abroad for treatment and you're back in UK. Partner's spending counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

response.reason: Child has been in hospital or residential accommodation for more than 12 weeks and you are not regularly spending money on them
response.guidance: Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital or residential accommodation, unless you regularly spend money on them. If child returns within 28 days of leaving, the time counts towards the 12-week limit. Contact HMRC to check if exceptions apply. Partner's spending only counts if married/civil partnered and living together.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (6 years old) meets all the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit: 1) He is under 16 years old, 2) He is not working 24+ hours per week, 3) He does not receive benefits in his own right, 4) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, 5) You (the claimant) live in the UK, 6) You are responsible for Alex as he lives with you, 7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for him, 8) He lives normally at home, and 9) The local council is not paying towards his accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (3 years old) is unfortunately not eligible for Child Benefit at this time because: 1) Blake has been in hospital for approximately 12 weeks (around 3 months), 2) Child Benefit continues for up to 12 weeks when a child is in hospital, 3) An exception to this 12-week rule exists if you are regularly spending money on the child while they are in hospital, but you confirmed you have not been able to afford to do so. Therefore, the hospital stay rule means Child Benefit cannot continue for Blake while they remain in hospital without you regularly spending on their needs.

response.overall_reasoning: This family has two children with different eligibility outcomes. Alex, who lives at home normally, is fully eligible for Child Benefit. Blake, who has been hospitalised for approximately 3 months (around 12 weeks), is not currently eligible because the hospital stay exceeds the 12-week limit and the claimant is not regularly spending money on Blake's needs during the hospital stay. When Blake returns home, they should become eligible for Child Benefit again.
